name: Fog of War
description: A map information system where terrain, units, resources, or enemy actions are hidden until revealed by scouts, sensors, ownership, or shared team vision.
design_notes: Fog of war creates strategy through uncertainty. The system should separate unexplored cells, explored-but-currently-hidden cells, visible cells, stale intel, team sharing, and replay state. UI and network authority must agree on what each player is allowed to know.
design_purpose: Make scouting, positioning, stealth, and map control strategically valuable.
Limit perfect information so opponents can maneuver, expand, or hide plans.
Give designers a structured way to reveal terrain, objectives, threats, and stale intelligence.
player_fantasy: The player feels they are building intelligence by sending units into uncertainty and watching the map come alive.

parameters.name: player_id
parameters.description: Player whose visibility state is being queried or updated.

parameters.name: team_id
parameters.description: Team used for shared vision and alliance rules.

parameters.name: fog_cell_id
parameters.description: Map cell storing explored, visible, or stale intelligence state.

parameters.name: visibility_state
parameters.description: Information band currently available for the cell.

parameters.name: revealed_at_tick
parameters.description: Tick when the cell most recently entered visible state.

parameters.name: scout_source_id
parameters.description: Source that contributed visibility to the cell.

parameters.name: stale_intel_policy_id
parameters.description: Rule for what remains visible after current sight is lost.

edge_cases: A unit dies or transfers ownership during the same tick it reveals enemy units.
Team alliances change and shared vision must update without leaking old enemy intel.
A replay or spectator view needs broader visibility than the active player had.
A stealthed unit enters a visible cell but should remain hidden without detection.
Late-joining multiplayer clients need fog state without receiving hidden enemy positions.
common_bugs: Minimap fog and world fog use different cell grids and show conflicting information.
Hidden enemy units are sent to clients and can be exposed by modded UI or memory inspection.
Visibility updates after combat resolution, allowing attacks from unrevealed targets.
Stale intel keeps updating enemy building health even while out of vision.
Replay save data stores only final fog state and cannot reconstruct scouting history.
balancing_notes: Scout radius, terrain blockers, and unit mobility define how expensive information is.
Team-shared vision can reduce coordination friction but may weaken scouting roles.
Stale intel is useful when it preserves planning context without revealing current enemy decisions.
accessibility_notes: Use texture, opacity, labels, or outlines in addition to color to distinguish fog states.
Keep minimap and main-map fog language consistent for players with low vision.
Provide readable alerts when important information becomes newly visible.

implementation_notes.unity: Store visibility per player or team in grid or chunk data keyed by fog_cell_id. Update scout sources in deterministic tick order, then render world masks and minimap overlays from the committed visibility_state without exposing hidden enemy entities to unauthorized clients.
implementation_notes.godot: Use TileMap or grid resources for fog cells and keep visibility state separate from rendered fog sprites. Multiplayer projects should replicate only allowed state per peer and avoid sending hidden unit data to clients that lack vision.
implementation_notes.unreal: Use replicated visibility components or server-side relevance rules for hidden actors. Fog rendering can use material masks, render targets, or grid textures, but authoritative visibility should stay in gameplay state for save and replay.
implementation_notes.web: Represent fog as per-player arrays or bitsets and draw masks on Canvas, WebGL, or DOM overlays. Keep visibility updates deterministic by sorting scout sources and cells before applying reveals.

relationships.reason: Fog of war needs minimap and map rendering to separate visible, explored, and hidden regions.
Territory ownership and fog visibility need rules for whether captured regions reveal current enemy activity.
Top-down cameras use fog cells to constrain visible tactical information.

example_games.title: StarCraft II
example_games.note: Uses fog of war and scouting as core strategic information systems.

example_games.title: Civilization VI
example_games.note: Uses exploration and map visibility to gate strategic knowledge.
